data.title: The S&P 500 Put The 'V' In November
data.description:
Stocks put the “V” in November. The S&P 500 (SP500) finished about flat for the month, which is an impressive feat, as the US large-cap index was –4.3% for the period just a week ago. The Nasdaq 100 (NDX) underperformed, losing 2%, while the Russell 2000 (RTY) posted a percentage point of alpha. Elsewhere, mid-caps scaled 2%.

Overseas stocks fared fine. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) was fractionally green as the US Dollar Index (DXY) wobbled around the 100 mark. Over in the bond market, the aggregate fixed income index returned just a bit under 1%.

In commodities, oil (CL1:COM) gave back 2%, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) soared 5%, and bitcoin (BTC-USD) plunged 17%. The entire crypto space stormed back over November’s final week, though. Bitcoin neared $80,000 at the low, only to reach $92,000 by Black Friday afternoon. Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) notched record highs on Black Friday.

November Returns: Gold Best, Bitcoin Worst, SPY Flat

Putting the "V" in November

Digging into the sector view, Health Care once again led. The pharma-heavy area of the S&P 500 jumped 9%, double the performance of the next best sectors (Materials and Consumer Staples).

Lagging was Information Technology. Nvidia (NVDA) dragged on the group, while shares of Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL) left something to be desired. To be fair, AAPL notched fresh record highs over the month’s final handful of sessions.

A standout tech stock was Broadcom (AVGO), which rose to new highs of its own. Oracle (ORCL), however, continued to sour, with shares giving back 23%.

S&P 500 Sector ETF Performances: Health Care Led in November, Tech Lagged

S&P 500 Performance Heat Map

Turning to the economy, the now-stale September jobs report revealed a fresh multi-year high in the unemployment rate, despite a better-than-expected headline payrolls gain. We should get the November employment data on time next Friday morning, but we may not know the precise state of the labor market for October.

Investors should also pay attention to private-sector jobs clues via next week’s PMI Employment subindexes, the JOLTS report, ADP Private Payrolls, jobless claims, and the November Challenger Job Cuts.

Unemployment Rate Rises to the Highest Since October 2021

The consumer is, of course, in focus on this Black Friday. Early indicators point to intense spending, even with higher price tags on the store shelves.

Once again, we don’t have the latest Retail Sales numbers from the US Census Bureau. We have in hand, however, the weekly Johnson Redbook retail sales data, and it shows a solid 5.9% YoY rise in total spending. Thanksgiving-Black Friday consumer trends point to even better gains.

Johnson Redbook +5.9% YoY

Early Black Friday Sales Robust

As for inflation, the September CPI report confirmed what macro onlookers already knew—the prevailing consumer-price rise is about 3% over the past 12 months. But US home values appear to be flat to in decline in some key regions. Hence, real-time inflation data (using timely shelter price data) point to inflation closer to 2.2%.

Back out tariffs (which are seen by some as transitory), and broader inflation appears tame. Just take a look at the inflation swaps market—traders price in 2.5% inflation across timeframes.

US CPI Holds Near 3%

Inflation Swaps Now Centered on 2.5%

Putting it all together, Q3 US real GDP growth appears to be headed for a 3.5% print (or better). Early looks at Q4 expansion are also sanguine.

All of this comes ahead of what should be a big tax-refund season, given the effects of President Trump’s One Big, Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). Expect spending to tick up beginning in February.

US Real GDP Growth Seen Near 3.7% For Q3

The lynchpin to the holiday-week rally to close out November was a dovish turn regarding Fed speak. NY Fed President John Williams cracked the door wide open to a December rate cut on Friday, November 21--the S&P 500 jumped and never looked back into the month-end.

The FOMC enters its blackout period tonight, and it appears likely that a quarter-point ease is in the cards come December 10.

A December Rate Cut Likely

Wrapping things up with the S&P 500 earnings trends, we see more optimistic data. Both 2025 and 2026 EPS estimates are now at multi-month highs.

That’s a somewhat unusual bullish trend, as earnings forecasts are often trimmed as the calendar year wears on. In fact, S&P 500 EPS is now above $300 on a forward-12-month basis for the first time.

S&P 500 EPS Estimates Rising

S&P 500 Forward EPS $306

December Often Is A Strong Month

December Returns...Back-Half Loaded, On Average

The Bottom Line

Stocks battled back to the flat line in November, led not by tech but by many blue chips. December has historically been a strong month, but gains are often back-half loaded. All eyes will be on the Fed on December 10, as well as tariff news and year-ahead outlooks.

data.title: Freehold Royalties Takes Texas Pacific Land To The Cleaners
data.description:
It has been almost a year since we penned this piece.

Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) has in the interim delivered almost exactly 35% down versus the 70% slide we were looking for.

Whoa, we're half way there

Whoa oh, livin' on a prayer

Hit the bid, we'll make it I swear

Whoa oh, livin' on a prayer

Source: Livin On A Prayer (Bear Edition)

In that piece and the sequel, we told investors just two things.

1) Avoid TPL, like the bubonic plague.

2) If you want royalty exposure, buy Freehold Royalties (FRU:CA). This long-short trade made you 52% over a year.

We review the recent results, valuation and tell you why we would look for a swing trade in TPL after year-end tax loss selling. We also go over our outlook for Freehold.

Q3-2025

TPL missed estimates by a mile and came in 50 cents below where the analyst community was aiming. The analyst community in this case is just one single, number cruncher, who probably has the least enviable task of figuring out production and commodity prices in advance

We are unsure as well as to what would have been a reasonable expectation but TPL did manage to deliver some goodies to the bulls. Production was up nicely across all 3 commodities and even realized pricing improved from a year ago. They key positive metric though was the quarter over quarter improvement, where production was up sharply.

One area which was a bit off though, was the surge in water-related expenses. But this has demonstrated a fair bit of variability over the last few years. Equally importantly, the year to date number for this expense wasn't particularly high. Management addressed the growth in the water division during the conference call.

Turning to our Water Services and Operations segment. This business rebounded considerably from last quarter. As I mentioned earlier, water sales revenue was a record for third quarter 2025. Although rigs and frac spreads have trended lower, upstream operators continue to prioritize development efficiencies as we see persistent deployment of co-completions and simul and trimul fracking. Our investments in brackish and treated water infrastructure have established TPL as one of the few systems in the Permian capable of accommodating the volume intensity required to keep up with operators.

On the produced water royalty side, revenues and volumes continue to perform well. Year-over-year, quarterly revenues and volumes were up 16% and 19%, respectively, as we see strong demand for both in-basin and out-of-basin pore space.

Source: TPL Q3-2025 Conference Call Transcript

Overall it was not a bad quarter, though it appeared like TPL missed.

TPL Tells You What It Is Worth

We've gotta hold on to what we've bought

It doesn't make a difference if they build a data center or not

We've got the water business and that's a lot for, 27X sales,

We'll give it a shot

Source: Livin On A Prayer (Bull Version)

TPL decided to address the "bubble" talk indirectly. They bought some new acreage.

To that end, yesterday we announced acquisitions of Permian oil and gas royalties and surface acreage which fits seamlessly into the broader TPL portfolio. On November 3, 2025, we acquired approximately 17,300 net royalty acres standardized to 1/8 located primarily in the Midland Basin in Martin, Howard and Midland Counties.

Total purchase price was approximately $474 million, funded entirely by cash on our balance sheet. Approximately 70% of the acquired interests are adjacent to or overlapping drilling spacing units that TPL already owns, meaning we essentially acquired additional royalties in current and future well locations we already retain an interest in. Approximately 61% of the royalty acreage is operated by Exxon, Diamondback and Occidental. The royalty acquisition currently produces more than 3,700 barrels of oil equivalent per day with an approximately 80% oil and natural gas liquids cut.

Source: TPL Q3-2025 Conference Call Transcript (emphasis ours)

The math is simple. TPL produces 36,300 barrels of oil equivalent a day. They just bought about one-tenth of that production for $474 million. Ergo, and hear us out before you cover your ears (we are looking at you who went all in at $1,700 a share), the total value of the company is about $4.74 billion. In stark contrast, here is where TPL trades.

You can see why we used every expletive (privately, SA has standards) while describing the company when it traded at $36 billion. Getting back to the current valuation, it still appears extremely high. It still appears extremely high if you build in doubling of oil and gas prices.

TPL Vs Freehold Royalties

Those that took our recommendation for switching to Freehold, we have some good news. The good news is that TPL still looks like a giant pile of junk relative to Freehold. There really is no reason to pay 18X extra on a sales multiple unless you are a real masochist looking for tax losses.

There is no reason to buy TPL based on the dividend yield either...unless you are indifferent to 0.74% and 7.22% dividend yield.

Our fair value for TPL is less than half the current price, but even if you go by the only analyst on Wall Street that has given an opinion, TPL trades at a 55% premium to NAV.

In contrast, Freehold is at a 9% discount.

Of course, the market could once again ignore valuation and give TPL investors a little extra hope. This is exactly what happened last year as there were a few rallies driven by news of data center buildouts on TPL land. That of course made zero sense as most of land owned by TPL is in extremely hostile environmental territory. Anyone considering building a data center there needs to have their head examined. But even leaving that lack of mental acuity aside, TPL owns less than 1% of the total acreage in Texas. So mathematically, if 100 data centers were built in Texas, one or two might be on TPL property. Oil and gas producing land commands some premium as shown in the recent acquisition price. Barren/grazing land is so cheap that we would assign 1-5% of total TPL NAV to that portion of their assets. Selling some to build a data center won't even pay the CEO's salary for the year. We think that the longer term path for TPL is firmly down, though the stock might stage a rebound post tax-loss selling. The stock is 21.3% below its 200 day moving average, and that has historically produced some incredible rallies.

So we are hesitant to put a "Strong Sell" on this at these levels. We would be happy to do so once it rises up enough to meet the 200 day moving average.

On a different note, we are moving Freehold to a "hold", as the Canadian energy stocks have just crushed their US brethren. We are finding incredible value deals in US energy (on a relative basis) for the first time in ages. Just not in TPL.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Oil Continues To Slump While Other Commodities Rally
data.description:
Oil may be down 13% this year, but commodities as a whole are climbing. Hussein Allidina, Managing Director and Head of Commodities at TD Asset Management, MoneyTalk to unpack the unusual split, why crude could be facing further declines and what investors should watch heading into 2026.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Commodities have been full of surprises this year. Oil prices are down about 13% year to date. Yet, a quick look at the space overall-- when you include commodities like gold, copper, natural gas, and others-- you'll see the sector is up by roughly the same amount. My next guest says that's because there's a lot going on beneath the surface. So as we head into 2026, what does that mean for the outlook for prices and portfolio construction?

Joining me now, Hussein Allidina, head of commodities at TD Asset Management. It is lovely to have you here.

Hussein Allidina: Thanks for having me.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with the chart, because that is going to illustrate. That's the reason I talked about what I did when I started this conversation. Let's bring it up, and you tell me what we're looking at here.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so this is a time series of the Bloomberg Commodity Index. That's the benchmark of the strategy that we manage. And it includes over 20 commodities. One-third of the basket is energy, so your crude, natural gas, gasoline, diesel, et cetera. One-third of the basket, roughly, is base and precious metals. And one-third is grain, softs and livestock. So it's a diversified basket of commodity exposure.

Generally, that's the benchmark that folks are looking at when they're thinking about portfolio construction and introducing commodities into their asset mix. When you read academic research, which is a ton of fun, nine times out of 10-- nine times out of 10, they're referring to this benchmark when they're talking about why one should have a commodity allocation in a diversified, balanced portfolio.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, well, up and to the right is always a good thing-- which it was.

Hussein Allidina: Well, look. I think this is interesting, because when I speak with clients, they're like, well, oil's off 15% year to date-- which is, to be clear, that's the spot price of oil, which is very different than if you were long oil and rolling futures. That return this year is only down 6% because the curve is in backwardation. But the key here is that commodities are not homogeneous. They all have different supply-demand balances.

What I think is homogeneous, though, for the commodities space is that capital has not been attracted into this space for the better part of 10-12 years. So we're starting to see the supply side across commodities tighten. There are exceptions. Today oil, I think, is oversupplied. But generally speaking, we've starved the space of capital. And, as a result, inventories are tightening because we continue to consume this stuff.

Kim Parlee: So I want to spend the first part talking about commodities within the portfolio. Second part, we'll talk about individual commodities. Tell me more about the starving of capital into the commodities space. And maybe how does that compare to previous periods and what you see?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so, commodities. So we have a ton of recency bias. Commodities, I guess, pre-2020 spent 10 years in this, what I call, exploitation phase. We were exploiting the investment that we made in the super-cycle which started in the early 2000s. The increase in price that we saw in the early 2000s brought about a tremendous amount of capital. We've joked about this before-- it's not Amazon Prime. It takes time before that capital that you've put into the ground lends to an increase in oil production, copper production.

Kim Parlee: Long time.

Hussein Allidina: Five, 10 years. So for the last 10, 15 years, we've been exploiting it because we made all that investment. And capital has not gone into the commodities space-- which is the right thing, because prices were weak and balances were oversupplied. Today, if we look at where capital is going, it's going into Mag 7, AI, data centers, hyperscalers. Those functions are very, very commodity-intensive. You need a lot of power to drive the language models. You need a lot of copper to move that power.

And unlike prior cycles-- we talked about the cycle which started in the '70s-- capital prior to that was going into the Nifty 50, the Kodak Polaroid. In the cycle that started in the 2000s, the period before that saw capital going into the internet stocks-- Pets.com, Mozilla, Netscape, et cetera. Those were not very commodity-intensive places. Today, capital is going into a space that's very commodity-intensive, and that space is taking capital away-- if capital is fixed-- away from commodities.

Commodities have started to work. Commodity returns have averaged north of 10% annualized since 2020. But that's still not as meaningful as the returns folks are generating in the AI space. And my concern is that where I'm spending money today is actually very, very commodity-intensive, and I'm not spending sufficient capital to grow my commodity supply base.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, and I think that's the interesting, I guess, conundrum when you're saying-- or not conundrum, opportunity from your perspective-- is that we're saying that AI needs lots of commodities, yet the commodities space has been starved of capital because AI's been sucking up all the capital. And so, it's almost this double reinforcing piece that you see where things could go.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah. So I think commodity prices and commodity returns would be positive and above average over the course of the next decade, irrespective of the AI, just because we've starved it of capital. And GDP continues to grow, so our consumption globally of commodities is growing. Now I have this additional fillip where I'm actually creating a new source of demand, and it's meaningful. So, I think the setup is actually quite interesting. And I don't think portfolios, Kim, are constructed to benefit from this new reality.

Kim Parlee: And you were saying that just before we get to the individuals. But that's not just specific, I'll say, of gold-- because we were talking about that-- and also to general commodities. What do you see in portfolios, versus what do you think should be true for portfolios?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so if I look at the last-- if I look at the 20 years into COVID, I would argue that period of depressed inflation, very low rates was the anomaly. If I look at 100 years of data, that 20 years into COVID, or really the 15 years post the financial crisis and the QE and financial repression, led to very weak rates, very low inflation. And the optimal portfolio for that environment is a portfolio of fixed income and equities, right?

You don't really need diversification because when inflation is low, fixed income and equities have a negative correlation. So you get your diversification there. You're not looking for inflation protection because there was no inflation. I don't think that we necessarily are in an environment where inflation is going to average the 5%, 6%, 7% that we saw after COVID. But I don't think it's a stretch to say that the 20 years into COVID was the anomaly. And it's likely that the volatility around both growth and inflation is going to be higher in the next 10 years.

And that portfolio-- if you're designing that portfolio-- it includes infrastructure, it includes real estate, it includes commodities, because you have to hedge those other risks that we didn't really face in that sort of pre-COVID period.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with oil. I know we said it's down while the rest of the complex is up. But why is it down?

Hussein Allidina: The balance is in surplus. So we, throughout the year, we're probably more conservative-- less bearish than consensus. Last year, got some weak data out of China, and the market writ off the idea of demand growth. Demand is growing. The world is going to see probably 900 to 1 million barrels a day of demand growth this year. But you have non-OPEC production increasing on the heels of the high prices that we saw in 2011-2012 and, to a lesser extent, some of the price appreciation we saw in 2022.

So, longer lead time projects in Brazil, Guyana, and Canada are coming to fruition. But at the same time, we do have Permian production and shale production starting to flatten and decline. OPEC is adding barrels back to the market. On paper, we have a surplus of somewhere between 2 and 4 million barrels a day, which is big. We're going to see big inventory builds, and that should pressure prices over the course of the next three to six months.

There's risk to this, Kim, of course. We have continuing geopolitics in the Middle East, continuing geopolitics in Russia, Ukraine, increased activity in the Caribbean. So there is risk, but on paper, your supply and demand looks bearish because of this increase in production. So we think that over the course of the near term, there is downside. We think you could trade down into the low 50s.

And ultimately, that does discourage the producers from deploying capital and we create an issue down the road. But today's focus is very much on this oversupplied balance. Inventories are building on the water, so you've seen a tremendous increase of oil on the water. At some point, that makes its way into onshore inventories and challenges the price and the structure, is our base case.

Kim Parlee: What about gold? And gold has had an incredible rally in the past little while. So, how much longer?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, look, I think structurally, the case for gold is very much this emerging market central bank buying that I'm probably putting you to death on, we've talked about it so many times. But emerging markets hold more aggregate reserves than developed markets. So I'm talking paper and gold in aggregate.

But when I look at their asset allocation, they're sitting in a lot of paper. The weighted average gold holding for the developed market central bank is around 60% of assets. The number for the emerging market is 10%, and this is after they've bought over 1,000 tons a year for the last three years, and after the price appreciation--

Kim Parlee: That's fascinating.

Hussein Allidina: So will those emerging market central banks stop buying gold? They might pause when you see a big rally in price. But it's hard for me to believe that directionally, the macro supports wanting to hold more dollars when you already have 90% of your reserves in dollars. So I think you'll continue to see that diversification that will continue to support gold. And I think you'll see retail institutional interest in gold that is a reflection of concerns around debasement, or Fed independence, or the performance of equities.

It's remarkable, actually, that gold has done what it's done this year--

Kim Parlee: Absolutely.

Hussein Allidina: - --notwithstanding the fact that equities have continued to perform.

Kim Parlee: Fascinating times. We've only got about three minutes. I'm trying to get this in, but critical minerals. And I know some of these are rare earths, critical minerals. This is not something that you trade and hold and those types of things. But what's been interesting, as you mentioned, is what's now been declared a critical mineral.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, yeah, so the US last week or the week before came out with an updated list on what's critical. Copper and nickel alloy are there. And I think this really just underscores the importance of commodities in making the world work.

Kim Parlee: National security.

Hussein Allidina: Well, and so the US has recognized that a lot of the things that we want to do requires these commodities. A lot of copper to build out the power generation, which has been underinvested in even before this sort of AI theme came to fruition. So I think this is an effort to underscore the importance of these commodities and to encourage domestic production.

Kim Parlee: Can I ask you about silver? I mean, it tends to track gold, but I know it has an industrial use as well, too.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, silver definitely has more of an industrial component than gold. We have invested throughout the year in silver from both the long and short side. We don't share the same sentiment to silver that we do to gold because we don't think that central banks are going to start buying silver. It's far more-- it's larger to store. So it doesn't have that benefit, but it does trade and will continue to trade, probably, with gold-- just a more volatile version.

Kim Parlee: I don't know if we're out of time, but we will. What about the grains and the soft commodities? What's your outlook for that?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so the grains broadly-- so grains and softs different. Grains have found quite a bit of price appreciation over the course of the last several weeks on anticipation of improved trade relations between China and the US. I think President Trump has probably tweeted specifically about soybeans more than a handful of times. When we look at the fundamental balances, China has been buying a lot of beans from Brazil, from Argentina over the course of the year.

They may buy some more beans from the US, I think, to appease President Trump. But they're not taking 20 million tons, we don't think, this year. If we look at crush margins in China, if we look at the size of the hog herd in China, it's all contracting, and they're sitting on a pretty good quantity of beans. So we think that rally in grains is probably one to be sold.

When we look at conditions in South America, Brazil, Argentina, estimates on their crops continue to get larger. I think there's been, again, a little bit of a relief rally on the heels of what might be more volume going from the US to China, but the data doesn't support it.

Kim Parlee: I've only got literally 10 seconds. This is unfair, but weather. Weather is so much more volatile than it used to be. How does that play into how you see things?

Hussein Allidina: It plays a meaningful role in natural gas, in particular. So, we like today owning European gas because we think it's not pricing any weather risk. The start of the weather has been-- the winter has been relatively mild, which is tempering things. We have a lot of winter ahead of us. Weather plays a role into our fundamental analysis. We assume normal weather. We will play on either side of that to see what happens. But ultimately, weather does play a role.

Original Post

data.title: IDEV Vs VOO: More Favorable Outlook For International Stocks Heading Into 2026
data.description:
Introduction

After a decade of underperformance leading up to 2024, international equities are finally outperforming their U.S. peers in 2025. The iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV) has delivered a total return of ~28% so far this year, markedly above the ~17% gain for the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO):

Looking ahead to 2026, I believe the outlook for incremental outperformance of IDEV remains intact. As discussed in my previous article on IDEV, the Buy case for international stocks is principally driven by lower valuations, as well as incremental weakening of the U.S. dollar amid ongoing Fed policy normalization. As such, I confirm my Buy rating on IDEV, driven by:

  • A trailing earnings yield of about 5.41%, more than compensating for the weaker 2026 GDP growth outlook of ~1.25% for countries where IDEV invests.
  • An overweight position in cyclical sectors such as financials, industrials, and materials, which is likely to deliver reasonable results amid steady economic expansion.
  • Better diversification and smaller reliance on AI/automation growth, as evidenced by the underweight position in the information technology sector.

In this article, I will compare the two ETFs based on sector exposure, GDP growth outlook, and current valuations, ultimately leading me to rate VOO a Hold. I also discuss risks in the investment case for the two ETFs.

ETF Comparison

You can access all IDEV details on the iShares website here. All VOO details are available on the Vanguard website here.

Both IDEV and VOO are low-cost ways to add exposure to developed market equities (expense ratios of 0.04% and 0.03% respectively). The principal difference is the geographic allocation of the two ETFs.

The iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF invests in large, mid, and small-capitalization stocks in developed markets outside the United States (the benchmark index is the MSCI WORLD ex USA IMI Index). In contrast, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF invests exclusively in the large-cap U.S. companies comprising the S&P 500 index.

From a geographic perspective, IDEV's largest allocation is to Japan (21.46% of assets), followed by the United Kingdom (12.72%), and Canada (11.81%):

While both ETFs are traded in USD, IDEV represents an implicit short bet on the U.S. dollar since its value depends on the stock prices of international companies traded in local currencies. That said, there are nuances when it comes to currency exposure. Since large-cap companies comprise the majority of both ETFs, these companies are naturally more export oriented.

As such, a weak U.S. dollar may boost IDEV's price in dollars but hurt underlying company fundamentals, resulting in a lower price in the local currency. Vice versa, a strong U.S. dollar may push down IDEV's price in dollars, but it is beneficial for underlying company fundamentals, potentially resulting in a higher price in local currency.

Sector Comparison

Before examining current valuations, it makes sense to compare the two ETFs from a sector allocation perspective, as shown in the table below:

Sector\ETF IDEV VOO Difference
Financials 23.83% 12.90% +10.93%
Industrials 18.18% 8.10% +10.08%
Consumer Discretionary 9.49% 10.50% -1.01%
Health Care 9.44% 9.00% +0.44%
Information Technology 8.74% 36.10% -27.38%
Materials 7.57% 1.70% +5.87%
Consumer Staples 6.87% 4.70% +2.17%
Energy 4.69% 2.80% +1.89%
Communication Services 4.08% 10.10% -6.02%
Utilities 3.50% 2.30% +1.20%
Real estate 2.96% 1.80% +1.16%
Cash/derivatives 0.65% 0% +0.65%

Source: Author's calculations

We observe that IDEV is principally overweight in cyclical sectors such as financials (+10.93% relative to VOO), industrials (+10.08%), and materials (+5.87%). At the same time, notable underweight positions include information technology (-27.38%) and communication services (-6.02%).

As such, outside of valuations which I will discuss below, the key difference in returns should come on two fronts. Firstly, if economic growth in countries where IDEV invests surprises on the upside/accelerates relative to 2025, we should see IDEV outperform. Secondly, if AI/automation growth comes in ahead of expectations, we should see VOO outperform.

Valuation Comparison

Now that we have observed the sector allocation of the two ETFs, we can put the current valuation of IDEV and VOO into perspective. IDEV trades at roughly 18.48x its trailing earnings, or an earnings yield of ~5.41%. VOO on the other hand trades at approximately 28.02x its trailing earnings, or an earnings yield of 3.57%. Hence we can conclude that VOO earnings would have to grow about 1.84% faster than IDEV just to make up for higher starting valuations.

Looking at the IMF's 2026 GDP growth outlook and weighing it against IDEV's country allocation, we see that countries in which IDEV invests should deliver GDP growth of about 1.25% in 2026:

Country Weight 2026 GDP Growth
Japan 21.46% 0.6%
United Kingdom 12.72% 1.3%
Canada 11.81% 1.5%
France 8.56% 0.9%
Germany 7.70% 0.9%
Switzerland 7.69% 1.3%
Australia 6.12% 2.1%
Netherlands 3.93% 1.2%
Sweden 3.36% 1.9%
Spain 3.03% 2.0%
Italy 2.81% 0.8%
Hong Kong 1.78% 2.1%
Denmark 1.69% 2.2%
Singapore 1.56% 1.8%
Israel 1.31% 3.9%
Belgium 1.05% 1.0%
Other/cash 3.40% N/A
Total ~100% 1.25%

Source: Author's calculations

The IMF expects the U.S. to deliver GDP growth of 2.1% next year, indeed higher than my 1.25% calculation for IDEV. Even so, the difference of 0.85% is not enough to make up for the higher earnings yield you are getting by investing in IDEV. In essence, all else equal, IDEV's current earnings yield of 5.41% is arguably better than a 3.57% earnings yield at VOO and a 0.85% faster growth rate stemming from the S&P 500's exposure to the U.S. economy.

Of course, as explained in the Sector Comparison section, IDEV's performance depends on a favorable economic backdrop which would allow the ETF's overweight position in cyclical sectors to deliver robust results. GDP growth of 1.25% in 2026 is certainly not exciting, but at least it is far from recessionary.

IDEV and VOO Rating

Taking into account current valuations, sector allocation, and 2026 growth expectations, I see better relative value in IDEV despite its recent outperformance, confirming my Buy rating on the ETF.

The path to VOO outperformance is more challenging in my opinion, as it would require a deterioration in global GDP growth and/or outperformance at VOO's overweight positions in information technology and communication services. As such, I would only rate VOO a Hold.

I also want to return to my previous point about currencies, elaborated on in the ETF Comparison section. While currency effects on both ETFs are nuanced, I do expect some marginal USD weakness (clearly not to the same extent as in 2025). Against this backdrop, the net effect in USD terms, should be incrementally higher returns for IDEV, partially offset by slower earnings growth.

To conclude, I see IDEV slightly outperforming VOO in 2026, driven largely by lower valuations, as well as a small boost from a weaker U.S. dollar.

Risks

For U.S. investors in IDEV, the two key risks I would highlight are: GDP growth falling short of expectations and secondly, the U.S. dollar strengthening contrary to my outlook. Both would likely result in IDEV underperforming VOO.

For VOO, we also have the principal risk of the U.S. economy not accelerating in 2026. Indeed we note that faster GDP and earnings growth are already reflected in VOO valuations, hence continued weakness would be a negative surprise.

VOO investors are also exposed to the risk of AI/automation growth falling short of expectations, as evidenced by the outsized exposure to the information technology sector.

IDEV exhibits better diversification, with current holdings amounting to 2,265 individual companies. As such, the allocation to the top ten companies stands at just 9.86% of ETF assets. In contrast, VOO's exposure to the ten largest positions accounts for roughly 39.65% of ETF assets.

Conclusion

After a decade of underperformance, international stocks have staged a comeback in 2025, benefiting from low starting valuations and a weak U.S. dollar. Looking ahead to 2026, I believe these two factors will continue to drive asset prices, resulting in further outperformance for IDEV relative to VOO.

I rate IDEV a Buy, as a higher earnings yield should more than compensate for weaker expected GDP growth. I rate VOO a Hold as the U.S.'s higher growth potential is arguably already reflected in current valuations.

The ultimate outcome in 2026 will largely depend on two factors, firstly whether international GDP growth meets expectations, and secondly how the outsized bet on AI/automation growth exhibited by VOO pans out.

U.S. investors in particular should pay attention to currency risk when investing in IDEV, although my base case is for a marginally weaker USD next year.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Ethereum Price Prediction: Buyers Defend $3k As Outflows Ease And Oversold Bounce Gains Traction (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Ethereum (ETH-USD) traded near $3,045 on Friday, extending its rebound from the long-term ascending trendline that has defined its structural base since late 2023. The recovery follows a sharp November breakdown that pushed ETH-USD into oversold territory and forced price into the lower Bollinger Band.

Sellers have lost momentum for now, but the broader downtrend remains intact, setting up a decisive test as the market enters the final sessions of the month. The question now is whether buyers can build a base or if ETH-USD slides back toward $2,900 and $2,750.

ETH rebounds from structural support as EMAs and bands tighten

The bounce developed at a precise confluence: the lower Bollinger Band and the multi-month rising trendline that has anchored ETH-USD’s broader structure for nearly a year. Price has reclaimed the short-term 20-day EMA at $3,109, a level that typically reflects early momentum shifts. The next hurdle lies at the descending trend line from November’s peak near $4,800, which now aligns with the 50-day EMA at $3,450. Clearing this area would signal the first meaningful break in the corrective structure.

ETH-USD price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

The Bollinger Bands show early signs of compression after an aggressive expansion during November’s selloff. ETH-USD still trades below the midpoint band at $3,569, and reclaiming that level would materially improve bullish conviction. Overhead, the 100-day EMA at $3,627 and the 200-day EMA at $3,504 form a dense resistance cluster that buyers must overcome to turn this rebound into a sustainable leg higher.

Flows show early relief. Spot outflows on November 28 registered $11.56M, still negative but significantly lighter than the heavy prints earlier this month. The reduction matches stabilization across derivatives, where open interest slipped only 0.72% to $36.78B. That suggests no major unwinding despite last week’s volatility. Total futures volume dropped almost 25%, pointing to cooling speculative activity rather than renewed panic.

Derivatives positioning and spot flows reveal a market searching for direction

The derivatives landscape shows a balanced but cautious market. Account-based long/short ratios lean bullish, with Binance traders at 1.81 and OKX at 1.51, while top-trader positioning on Binance shows a stronger tilt at 2.89. But options flows tell a different story. Options open interest fell 18.7%, and volume declined more than 31%, signalling weak hedging demand and limited conviction from institutional traders.

Liquidations reinforce the split picture. In the past 24 hours, longs absorbed $18M in liquidations while shorts took $20.97M, showing that neither side holds dominant control and that markets remain sensitive to small shifts in flows.

Spot flows remain ETH-USD’s biggest obstacle. November has recorded persistent red prints, reflecting steady investor distribution across the month. The cumulative supply leaving exchanges aligns with ETH-USD’s failure to defend the $3,500-3,800 range earlier in November. Reversing these outflows is essential for ETH-USD to mount a sustained recovery.

Structurally, Ethereum sits inside a narrowing wedge formed by the long-term ascending base and the sharp descending trendline from the peak. This compression typically precedes decisive breaks. A daily close above $3,450 would signal a transition toward testing $3,800, followed by the psychological $4,000 barrier. Failure to clear this zone keeps downside risk open toward $2,900, with a deeper move toward $2,750 if outflows accelerate.

For now, ETH-USD is stabilizing but has not shifted the broader trend. RSI has lifted from oversold levels, selling pressure has eased, and the long-term trendline continues to hold. But with resistance stacked overhead and spot flows still negative, buyers must demonstrate sustained momentum to convert this rebound into something larger.

In earlier coverage, we noted ETH’s vulnerability after losing the $3,500-3,800 band and warned that persistent spot outflows placed pressure on any recovery attempt. The current rebound confirms those concerns while showing early signs of stabilization at long-term support.

data.title: Empire Petroleum: Likely Needs Significant Additional Funding In 2026
data.description:
Empire Petroleum (EP) reported relatively positive Q3 2025 results. It reduced its lease operating expense per BOE by 13% compared to Q2 2025 and also increased its oil production by 5% quarter-over-quarter with minimal in-quarter capex.

This allowed Empire to generate slightly positive ($0.1 million) adjusted EBITDA during the quarter with mid-$60s WTI oil. I now expect it to end 2025 with around $4 million to $5 million in liquidity, which is several million better than I had previously expected.

That being said, Empire's situation remains challenging with 2026 oil strip prices that are still below $60. At that oil price, it is likely to generate negative adjusted EBITDA despite its progress with reducing lease operating costs.

It also has a bank credit facility that currently matures in December 2026 (with $14.1 million currently outstanding), and that credit facility may have minimal availability remaining by late 2026.

I am reducing my estimate of Empire's value from $4.50 to $3.75 per share in a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment due to concerns over its liquidity and the potential dilution needed to address its liquidity.

Empire is pretty risky due to its likely need to raise significant additional funding in 2026 while its share price is relatively weak. I am thus remaining neutral on it despite some potential upside in the right circumstances.

Q3 2025 Results

Empire increased its production slightly in Q3 2025. It averaged 2,398 BOEPD in total production, which was a 2% quarter-over-quarter increase. Empire's oil cut went from 63% in Q2 2025 to 65% in Q3 2025, resulting in a 5% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil production (to 1,566 barrels of oil per day).

Empire's lease operating expense went down again to $25.99 per BOE in Q3 2025 due to lower workover costs. This was a 13% decrease compared to Q2 2025 and a 17% reduction compared to Q1 2025. It is uncertain whether this trend will continue or whether Empire's workover costs will rebound upwards a bit, but there has been good progress there.

The lease operating expense reductions and the increased oil production (as well as a slight increase in oil prices compared to Q2 2025) allowed Empire to generate $0.1 million in positive adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025. This was better than I expected, as well as being a $1.3 million improvement in adjusted EBITDA compared to Q2 2025.

Empire's reported capex for the quarter (on an accrual basis) appears to be approximately $0.9 million.

Increased Focus On Natural Gas

Empire is looking at increasing its focus on natural gas development in 2026 due to the combination of relatively weak oil prices and strengthening natural gas prices. The oil-to-natural gas price ratio is currently expected to be around 14 to 1 in 2026 based on current strip prices, compared to a more typical 20 to 1 ratio. This ratio was 35 to 1 in 2024 with relatively strong oil prices and quite weak natural gas prices.

Thus, Empire is advancing its East Texas development program, targeting an early 2026 start for drilling operations there. It intends to utilize around 12 DUC wells there to help accelerate its natural gas development and expects that natural gas will play a larger role in its results in 2026.

Notes On Cash Position

Empire completed its subscription rights offering in Q3 2025, raising $2.5 million in gross proceeds. This contributed to Empire ending Q3 2025 with $4.6 million in cash on hand, up from $2.3 million at the start of the quarter.

While Empire's Q3 2025 results were better than I expected, oil prices are currently lower in Q4 2025 by around $5 to $6 per barrel compared to Q3 2025. Natural gas prices have improved quarter-over-quarter, but natural gas only accounts for 16% of Empire's production. Thus, its realized price per BOE will likely go down by approximately $3 in Q4 2025. This would translate into negative $0.5 million adjusted EBITDA for the quarter if its production and lease operating costs remain stable quarter-over-quarter.

Thus, Empire may end 2025 with around $2 million in cash on hand along with approximately $2.5 million in credit facility availability. This assumes $2.1 million in capex and interest costs during Q4 2025 along with no other changes in working capital. Empire had an $8.6 million working capital deficit at the end of Q3 2025, although it has typically carried some working capital deficit, so that isn't unusual.

Liquidity Needs And Credit Facility Maturity

Empire had $14.1 million borrowed under its credit facility as of the end of Q3 2025. This credit facility had $3.3 million in unused commitments at the end of Q3 2025, but the commitment is decreasing by $0.25 million per month.

Thus, with no change to its credit facility borrowings (from $14.1 million), Empire will have no availability remaining by late 2026. The credit facility also currently matures on December 29, 2026.

As noted above, Empire is likely to generate slightly negative adjusted EBITDA at current 2026 strip prices and is also needing to fund its 2026 development plans. Thus, I can see Empire needing another $10+ million in cash during 2026 even if it can extend its credit facility maturity (or enter into a new agreement) with a similar amount of availability. If its credit facility availability is further reduced, then Empire would need to raise additional funds.

Notes On Valuation

While Empire's operational performance beat expectations in Q3 2025, I am still reducing my estimate of its value from $4.50 per share to $3.75 per share at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I had noted before that there could be negative impacts to Empire's value from any steps it needed to take to address its liquidity. Now it is potentially looking at needing to raise approximately $10 million to $25 million in cash during 2026 (depending on what happens with its credit facility) while its share price is at $3.

Empire could have some decent upside if oil prices recover to $70+ for 2027 and it manages to address its nearer-term liquidity needs without too much dilution. Empire's estimated liquidity needs during 2026 are a significant concern, though, and thus I am remaining neutral on it.

Conclusion

Empire's Q3 2025 results were fairly good, delivering 5% quarter-over-quarter production growth along with a 13% reduction in lease operating expense per BOE. This allowed it to generate $0.1 million in positive adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025 with mid-$60s WTI oil.

While Empire has performed operationally above my expectations, its situation remains quite challenging at the current high-$50s WTI oil strip for 2026. It is attempting to focus more on natural gas development with more favorable prices there. However, I still expect it to generate negative adjusted EBITDA in 2026, and it also has its credit facility maturing in December 2026 (with $14.1 million currently outstanding).

If Empire's 2026 capex is near $10 million, it will probably need to raise around $10 million to $25 million during 2026, depending on what happens with its credit facility. This could result in substantial dilution given that its share price is around $3 now.

data.title: 5 Top Stocks For AI Fatigue
data.description:
AI Bubble Fears On The Rise

Fears of an AI bubble fueled volatility in recent weeks as big tech valuations stretched and Wall Street’s top executives warned of a market correction. The S&P 500’s AI-led rally sent prices to record highs last month, but a global tech sell-off has the index on track for its weakest November since 2008. The S&P 500 clawed back some losses in recent sessions, but several underlying indicators suggest market sentiment remains cautious.

Global AI-related investments are projected to exceed $4 trillion by 2030, reflecting investor zeal to capitalize on a technology that surged in popularity after ChatGPT was launched three years ago. Record spending by big tech persists despite skepticism about a near-term payoff as many organizations report zero measurable return from billions in capital investment. Tech sector valuations have soared to dot-com era levels - and, based on price-to-sales ratios, well beyond them. Some of AI’s biggest proponents acknowledge the fact that valuations are overinflated, including OpenAI chairman Bret Taylor:

AI will transform the economy… and create huge amounts of economic value in the future,” Taylor told The Verge. “I think we’re also in a bubble, and a lot of people will lose a lot of money.”

Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Sundar Pichai sees “elements of irrationality” in the current scale of AI investing, not unlike the excessive investment during the dot-com boom. If the AI bubble bursts, Pichai added, no company will be immune, including Alphabet, despite its breakthrough technology, Gemini, fueling gains.

In addition to stretched valuations, the way big tech is leaning heavily on circular deals and heavy debt to finance AI spending has raised eyebrows on Wall Street. The top five hyperscalers have raised a record $108B worth of debt in 2025 - more than 3x the average over the past nine years, according to data compiled by Bloomberg Intelligence. Moreover, some firms are turning to off-balance-sheet entities, including special-purpose vehicles (SPVs) - structures with a checkered history going back to Enron, D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria observed. A slowdown in AI demand could render the debt tied to these SPVs “worthless,” Luria warned, potentially triggering another financial crisis.

Beyond valuations, AI’s rapid expansion has masked some underlying economic issues. As US investment in AI-related industries surged over the past five years, non-AI investment remained stagnant, a Deutsche Bank research report revealed. And, outside of data centers, economic demand has been weak. In fact, the US would be close to a recession this year without technology-related spending, the report added. Meanwhile, a front-loading of chip orders has helped offset some of the tariff-driven constraints on global trade.

Q3 results, however, may signal a shift, with strength spilling over into areas outside the AI theme. The muted reaction to earnings beats — and sharp selloffs after misses — may indicate that investors are growing weary of AI-driven narratives, according to BlackRock analysts:

We feel this may suggest signs of investor AI fatigue. Concerns surrounding risks in private credit, a hawkish Fed pivot, and AI bubbles all emerged over the course of earnings season, likely spurring a bout of profit taking in select companies.”

Investors seeking to minimize AI bubble risk can diversify by looking at stocks outside the tech sector that also have strong investment fundamentals.

Top Stocks For AI Fatigue

SA Quant identified five Strong Buy-rated stocks with lower exposure to AI bubble risks, spanning the energy, materials, real estate, healthcare, and industrial sectors. Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings offer a data-driven evaluation of each stock by comparing more than 100 metrics to sector peers and assigning grades across five factors: Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions.

1. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR)

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 2 out of 216

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 18

The No. 2 quant-rated energy stock, Par Pacific Holdings operates a vertically integrated refining, logistics, and retail platform that supplies conventional and renewable fuels across niche markets, including Hawaii, the Rockies, and the Pacific Northwest. A member of the PRO Quant Portfolio, PARR has a system-wide refining capacity of 219K bpd, an integrated logistics network with 13 MMbbls of storage, and marine, rail, and pipeline assets, in addition to 119 fuel retail locations. The stock has crushed the market and energy sector in the past year, driving an A+ Momentum Grade, and has topped earnings expectations for three straight quarters.

PARR 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Energy Sector (XLE)

The company has a 10-year track record of successful acquisitions aimed at increasing its refining scale and market reach. In recent years, PARR has also benefited from its investment in renewable fuels and joint ventures, adding new cash flow streams and hedging against energy transition and regulatory risk.​ PARR reported exceptional Q3 2025 results, with adjusted EPS outpacing estimates by $3.99 and revenue of $2.01B exceeding projections by $250M. The company achieved near-record system-wide throughput of 198K bpd in the quarter. Q3 results were bolstered by a small refinery exemption gain of $202.6M, while $100M in proceeds were achieved from the closing of the Hawaii Renewables joint venture with Mitsubishi and ENEOS.

Outstanding growth in EBITDA and operating cash flow in the past year underpin an A+ Growth Grade. Although EBITDA margins have dragged on its factor grade, PARR showcases excellent return on equity and total capital. Despite soaring momentum, the stock is trading at an attractive valuation with a P/E of 7x, representing a 47% discount to the sector. PARR is a niche energy player with a proven track record of profitable growth, excellent momentum, and my top Strong Buy pick for hedging AI bubble risk.

2. Guardant Health, Inc. (GH)

  • Market Capitalization: $13.95B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Health Care

  • Industry: Health Care Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 5 out of 936

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 48

No. 1 among quant-rated Healthcare Services stocks, Guardant Health is a precision oncology company that develops blood-based and tissue-based genomic tests to detect, monitor, and guide treatment for cancer. Guardant has shown excellent momentum in the past year, surging after beating Q3 earnings and boosting its full-year outlook.

Guardant 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Health Care Sector (XLV)

Guardant's top line rose nearly 39% YoY in Q3 to $265M as the company surpassed $1B in annualized revenue for the first time. Guardant saw strong performance across all business segments: Oncology, bipharma, and screening, led by stellar volume growth in the Guardant360 Liquid, Guardant360 Tissue, and Reveal product lines. Guardant solid gross margins expanded further in the prior-year period. This improvement was primarily driven by a significant reduction in Reveal COGS, which fell from $1,000 per test to $500 per test.

Over the past year, Guardant has delivered robust growth in revenue, which is expected to rise from $739M in FY 2024 to $1.97B by FY 2028, according to consensus estimates. Guardant has positive momentum in Wall Street analyst estimates as reflected by 15 upward earnings revisions in the past three months and just one down revision. A high-momentum healthcare stock with high long-term growth prospects is a great addition for balancing a portfolio.

3. Omega Healthcare Investors, Inc. (OHI)

  • Market Capitalization: $14.04B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Health Care REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 3 out of 174

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 17

Omega Healthcare Investors is a high-yielding, top quant-ranked healthcare REIT with more than 1,000 properties in the United States and UK and $11.4B in total real estate investments, focused on skilled nursing and assisted living. OHI invests in long-term healthcare facilities to generate profitable shareholder returns by offering triple-net leases and fixed-rate mortgage loans to a diverse group of healthcare operators.

OHI 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Real Estate Sector (XLRE)

In Q3 2025, OHI beat earnings estimates for the eighth straight quarter with adjusted FFO per share of $0.78, beating by $0.02, and revenue of $311.59M (+12.9% YoY), beating by $50M. Results were driven by an increase in facility acquisitions, lease extensions, and other rent escalations. OHI increased its full-year guidance, supported by improving occupancy and coverage, CEO Taylor Pickett explained in a release:

We again delivered strong quarterly results, as we continue to grow FAD per share while further de-levering the balance sheet,” Pickett said. "The backdrop continues to be favorable. Operating metrics remain strong, with both occupancy and coverage further improving in the quarter. The pipeline is very active, and we have a strong balance sheet and cost of capital that should allow us to continue to accretively invest."

OHI’s AFFO/revenue margin of over 80% underpins its ‘A’ Profitability Grade, along with robust cash flow and return on capital. OHI EPS is projected to grow by 7% in FY25 and revenue by 22% to $1.07B. OHI’s dividend has an excellent yield and is expected to continue growing in the next three years, according to consensus estimates. OHI offers a dividend yielding 5.85%, for a B+ Dividend Yield Grade, with 21 consecutive years of payments. A high payout ratio weighs on OHI’s dividend safety score, although the REIT maintains solid interest coverage and conservative leverage.

OHI is trading in line with the sector at a P/AFFO of 15x - but a solid dividend, robust AFFO margins, and high momentum make it a Strong Buy REIT.

4. Ardagh Metal Packaging S.A. (AMBP)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.24B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Metal, Glass & Plastic Container Stocks

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 34 out of 267

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 2 out of 10

A global can manufacturer with leading market positions in Europe, North America, and Brazil, Ardagh has steadily grown revenue and EBITDA over the past three years, benefiting from secular tailwinds, as aluminum cans continue to gain share of the global beverage packaging mix. AMBP has held a Strong Buy or Buy quant rating since the end of April, driven by consistently solid profitability, EPS revision, and valuation factor grades.

Ardagh 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Materials Sector (XLB)

Q3 revenue rose 8% to $1.43B and EBITDA +6% to $208M, driven by shipment growth in Europe and North America, lower operational costs, and a favorable category mix. Global volumes were up 2%, driven by growth in energy drinks, ciders, ready-to-drink teas and coffees, wines and water - offsetting weakness in the beer category. Ardagh expects low single-digit market growth in 2026, although it projects potential headwinds from the tariff situation in the next 12 to 18 months. Revenue growth has been solid in the past year, although the company’s growth grade has been dragged down by an inconsistent dividend. Ardagh is expected to continue growing net income and revenue over the next three years, and has six upward earnings revisions and one downward revision.

Trading at 5.9x cash flow, a 37% discount from the sector, is supporting an A+ Valuation Grade. Ardagh offers a dividend yielding 10.67%, although it suffers from weak safety, growth, and consistency. Ardagh has solid growth prospects and is riding green metal structural tailwinds, with low exposure to AI trends.

5. Global Ship Lease, Inc. (GSL)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.25B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Marine Transportation

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 4 out of 596

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/28/2025): 1 out of 24

The No. 1 quant-rated Marine Transportation stock and recent addition to the PRO Quant Portfolio, GSL is a high-yielding, highly profitable small-cap security that has crushed the market in the past year and is trading at a significant discount to the sector. Headquartered in Athens, Greece, GSL owns and charters mid-sized and smaller containerships to major global shipping companies.

GSL 1Y Price Return vs. S&P 500 Industrial Sector (XLI)

GSL delivered solid financial results in Q3 2025 with revenue of $192.7M (+10% YoY) and normalized EPS of $2.62 (+8.3% YoY), marking an eighth straight earnings beat. GSL’s $1.92B contract backlog helps shield against market volatility and includes 100% forward contract cover for 2025, 96% for 2026, and 74% for 2027. Despite the cyclicality of marine transportation, Global Ship Lease is capitalizing on headwinds and trade policy uncertainty - including new emissions rules and last-minute deferral of US and Chinese port surcharges, as highlighted by Executive Chairman George Youroukos in the latest earnings call:

All of these real and potential factors are contributing to two major effects, both of which play to our advantage. Number one, making supply chains less efficient, which means that more ships are needed to transport a given quantity of cargo; and number two, increasing the value of flexible, midsized and smaller container ships such as those in our fleet.”

GSL’s A+ Profitability Grade is underpinned by exceptional operating margins, return on equity and total capital, along with impressive capex/sales. GSL offers a stable and growing dividend that's yielding 7.13%, and showcasing a safe payout ratio of 20.26%. GSL’s dividend has a 3Y CAGR of 15.6%, supporting an ‘A’ Dividend Growth Grade. GSL reinstituted dividend payments in 2021 after suspending them in 2016 due to the severe downturn in the container shipping industry.

GSL is trading at a mere 3.4x earnings, an 83% discount to the sector, for an A+ Valuation Grade. Although the Marine Transportation industry can be volatile amid rapidly changing geopolitical and global factors, GSL is well positioned to navigate complexities while maximizing strategic optionality in both fleet deployment and balance sheet management. In addition, bullish momentum, strong profitability, and an excellent valuation make GSL a great pick to wind up my list of top stocks for investors looking for protection if the AI bubble bursts.

Best Stocks for Hedging AI Fatigue

AI bubble fears have fueled market volatility as tech capital spending hits record levels, valuations get stretched, and the much-hyped technologies fail to deliver returns for organizations. AI investments could reach $4 trillion by 2030, just as echoes of the dot-com era have Wall Street leaders warning of a market correction. Even AI proponents acknowledge the risk of the AI bubble bursting - a development that would spare no one, even big tech giants.
